title: Why we should vaccinate wild animals - Works in Progress Magazine
gist: Oral bait vaccination eliminated wildlife rabies in Switzerland and large parts of the US at large cost savings, and the same approach is now being extended to conservation (koalas, bats) and animal welfare, showing wild animals can be immunized at scale.

themes.theme: Zoonotic disease control at the source

insights.claim: Around 60 percent of infectious diseases are zoonotic in origin, with the majority originating from wildlife rather than livestock or pets, making wild-animal vaccination a potential way to prevent human disease before spillover occurs.
insights.speaker: Works in Progress
insights.so_what: Source-level wildlife immunization is a structurally cheaper pandemic-prevention lever than reactive human response; watch biotech and public-health programs targeting reservoirs.
insights.quote: Around 60 percent of infectious diseases are zoonotic in origin, meaning they are transmitted from animals to humans, and the majority of them originate from wildlife rather than livestock or pets.
insights.locator: But defense doesn't have to be adversarial

insights.claim: Because rabies does not spread between humans and infects them only incidentally, eliminating the disease reservoir in animals is far more cost-effective than vaccinating humans.
insights.speaker: Works in Progress
insights.so_what: Reservoir elimination economics generalize to other zoonoses, favoring upstream intervention over downstream treatment spending.
insights.quote: it's far more cost-effective to wipe out the disease reservoir in animals than to vaccinate humans
insights.locator: Since rabies doesn't spread between humans

themes.theme: Rabies eradication track record and economics

insights.claim: US dog-mediated rabies fell from 74 reported human deaths in 1921 to single digits per year by the 1960s after laws mandated dog vaccination, and the canine rabies variant was officially declared eradicated from the US in 2007.
insights.speaker: Works in Progress
insights.so_what: Demonstrates that mandated animal vaccination can effectively eliminate a disease vector within decades, a template for other programs.
insights.quote: from 74 reported human deaths across the country in 1921 to single digits per year by the 1960s
insights.locator: Nevertheless, wild animal vaccination first emerged

insights.claim: In Texas, every dollar spent on the coyote oral rabies vaccination program saved an estimated $4 to $13 in human post-exposure treatment, which cost around $3,500 per person.
insights.speaker: Works in Progress
insights.so_what: A documented 4x-13x ROI on a public-health intervention illustrates the fiscal case for scaling preventive wildlife programs.
insights.quote: every dollar spent on the coyote ORV program saved an estimated $4 to $13 in human treatment

insights.claim: The USDA National Rabies Management Program now distributes eight to ten million vaccine baits annually across the US, and the raccoon program created a rabies barrier at the Appalachian Mountains that has held for decades.
insights.speaker: Works in Progress
insights.so_what: Proven scale of bait distribution shows the logistics and manufacturing footprint already exists for mass wildlife immunization.
insights.quote: the USDA National Rabies Management Program distributes eight to ten million vaccine baits annually across the US
insights.locator: By 2009, the gray fox variant followed

themes.theme: Delivery innovation as the key constraint

insights.claim: The breakthrough in wildlife rabies control came in 1971 when Baer's CDC team found that pipetting an attenuated rabies virus vaccine into foxes' mouths produced immunity, enabling an oral bait approach rather than injection.
insights.speaker: Works in Progress
insights.so_what: Delivery method, not vaccine science, is the binding constraint in wildlife immunization; species-specific bait design is where value is created.
insights.quote: in 1971, Baer's team discovered that pipetting an attenuated rabies virus vaccine, where the virus has been rendered harmless without being killed, into the mouths of foxes produces immunity
insights.locator: The breakthrough came when Baer's group pivoted

insights.claim: Switzerland eliminated fox-mediated rabies entirely by 1999 after scattering millions of vaccine-laden chicken heads, including airdrops over the Swiss Alps by helicopter, following an initial 1978 trial of 4,000 baits at Lake Geneva.
insights.speaker: Works in Progress
insights.so_what: National elimination is achievable within roughly two decades once delivery is solved, setting a realistic timeline for other programs.
insights.quote: By 1999, fox-mediated rabies was eliminated from Switzerland entirely.
insights.locator: Following this success, Switzerland scaled up

insights.claim: White-nose syndrome has killed over six million North American bats since 2007, with three bat species declining by over 90 percent, and the USGS estimates bats contribute billions of dollars to the US agricultural economy annually through pest control.
insights.speaker: Works in Progress
insights.so_what: Bat collapse carries quantifiable agricultural and disease-control costs, framing bat vaccines as an economic, not just conservation, intervention.
insights.quote: the USGS estimates that bats contribute billions of dollars to the US agricultural economy annually
insights.locator: Vaccines for other diseases are still in the pipeline

insights.claim: In September 2025 Australia approved the world's first chlamydia vaccine for koalas, developed over a decade by Peter Timms, with a trial showing it cut koala mortality by around 65 percent.
insights.speaker: Works in Progress
insights.so_what: First-of-kind wildlife disease vaccines are now reaching regulatory approval, signaling a maturing pipeline of species-specific veterinary products.
insights.quote: In September 2025, Australia approved the world's first chlamydia vaccine for koalas.
insights.locator: Wild animal vaccines are emerging as a promising tool

insights.claim: Researchers are field-trialing a vaccine gel or paste applied to roosting bats that the animals spread through grooming, with epidemiologists running trials at a Wyoming site since September 2025 because bats cannot be lured with food baits.
insights.speaker: Works in Progress
insights.so_what: Self-spreading transmissible delivery methods are an active frontier; success would dramatically lower the cost of immunizing hard-to-reach wild populations.
insights.locator: So researchers are experimenting with novel delivery methods
